Appendix

Appendix A Proof of Theorem 4.1

We first introduce the necessary definitions and then proceed with the proof.

Definition A.1 (Binary Symmetric Channel).

A Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) with crossover (error) probability ϵ∈[0,1]\epsilon\in[0,1] is a memoryless channel where each input symbol is flipped independently with probability ϵ\epsilon. For input Y∈{+1,−1}Y\in\{+1,-1\} and output X∈{+1,−1}X\in\{+1,-1\}, we have:

X=Y⋅Z,where ​Z={+1with probability ​1−ϵ,−1with probability ​ϵ.X=Y\cdot Z,\quad\text{where }Z=\begin{cases}+1&\text{with probability }1-\epsilon,\\ -1&\text{with probability }\epsilon.\end{cases} (15)

Equivalently, we express the error probability as ℙ​(X≠Y)=ϵ\mathbb{P}(X\neq Y)=\epsilon.

Definition A.2 (Stochastic Degradation).

A (communication) channel 𝒞′\mathcal{C}^{\prime} is said to be a stochastically degraded version of channel 𝒞\mathcal{C} if there exists an intermediate channel 𝒞′′\mathcal{C}^{\prime\prime} such that the cascade 𝒞→𝒞′′\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}^{\prime\prime} is statistically equivalent to 𝒞′\mathcal{C}^{\prime}. In other words, the output of 𝒞′\mathcal{C}^{\prime} can be obtained by further corrupting the output of 𝒞\mathcal{C} through an independent noise process.

Lemma A.3 (BSC Degradation).

Let 𝒞1\mathcal{C}_{1} be a BSC with error rate ϵ1\epsilon_{1} and 𝒞2\mathcal{C}_{2} be a BSC with error rate ϵ2\epsilon_{2} where ϵ1≤ϵ2<1/2\epsilon_{1}\leq\epsilon_{2}<1/2. Then, 𝒞2\mathcal{C}_{2} is a stochastically degraded version of 𝒞1\mathcal{C}_{1}. Specifically, 𝒞2\mathcal{C}_{2} can be constructed by cascading 𝒞1\mathcal{C}_{1} with an independent BSC having crossover probability:

δ=ϵ2−ϵ11−2​ϵ1.\delta=\frac{\epsilon_{2}-\epsilon_{1}}{1-2\epsilon_{1}}. (16)
Proof.

Let Y∈{+1,−1}Y\in\{+1,-1\} be the input, Z1Z_{1} be the output of 𝒞1\mathcal{C}_{1}, and Z2Z_{2} be the output after passing Z1Z_{1} through a BSC with crossover probability δ\delta.

For the cascade, Z1=Y⋅N1Z_{1}=Y\cdot N_{1} where ℙ​(N1=−1)=ϵ1\mathbb{P}(N_{1}=-1)=\epsilon_{1}, and Z2=Z1⋅N2Z_{2}=Z_{1}\cdot N_{2} where ℙ​(N2=−1)=δ\mathbb{P}(N_{2}=-1)=\delta. Thus, we have:

Z2=Y⋅N1⋅N2.Z_{2}=Y\cdot N_{1}\cdot N_{2}. (17)

The overall error occurs when N1⋅N2=−1N_{1}\cdot N_{2}=-1, i.e., exactly one of N1N_{1} or N2N_{2} equals −1-1. Then, we write the error probability for the channel C2C_{2} as:

ℙ​(Z2≠Y)\displaystyle\mathbb{P}(Z_{2}\neq Y) =ℙ​(N1=+1)​ℙ​(N2=−1)+ℙ​(N1=−1)​ℙ​(N2=+1)\displaystyle=\mathbb{P}(N_{1}=+1)\mathbb{P}(N_{2}=-1)+\mathbb{P}(N_{1}=-1)\mathbb{P}(N_{2}=+1) (18)
=(1−ϵ1)​δ+ϵ1​(1−δ)\displaystyle=(1-\epsilon_{1})\delta+\epsilon_{1}(1-\delta) (19)
=δ−ϵ1​δ+ϵ1−ϵ1​δ\displaystyle=\delta-\epsilon_{1}\delta+\epsilon_{1}-\epsilon_{1}\delta (20)
=ϵ1+δ​(1−2​ϵ1).\displaystyle=\epsilon_{1}+\delta(1-2\epsilon_{1}). (21)

Setting this equal to ϵ2\epsilon_{2} and solving for δ\delta yields (16). Since ϵ1≤ϵ2<1/2\epsilon_{1}\leq\epsilon_{2}<1/2, we have δ∈[0,1]\delta\in[0,1], confirming the validity of this construction. ∎

Definition A.4 (Top-kk Ensemble).

Given mm channels indexed by increasing error rates ϵ1≤ϵ2≤⋯≤ϵm\epsilon_{1}\leq\epsilon_{2}\leq\cdots\leq\epsilon_{m}, the top-kk ensemble is defined as Hk={1,2,…,k}H_{k}=\{1,2,\ldots,k\}, containing the kk channels with the smallest error rates.

We now restate and prove the main theorem.

Theorem 4.1 [Alignment of Error and Mutual Information] Consider an ensemble of mm independent BSCs where channel jj has error rate ϵj<1/2\epsilon_{j}<1/2, indexed such that ϵ1≤ϵ2≤⋯≤ϵm\epsilon_{1}\leq\epsilon_{2}\leq\cdots\leq\epsilon_{m}. For any subset S⊆[m]S\subseteq[m] with |S|=k|S|=k, the top-kk set HkH_{k} satisfies:

I​(Y;𝐗𝐇𝐤)\displaystyle I(Y;{\bf X_{H_{k}}}) ≥I​(Y;𝐗𝐒),\displaystyle\geq I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}), (22)
ℙe​(Hk)\displaystyle\mathbb{P}_{e}(H_{k}) ≤ℙe​(S).\displaystyle\leq\mathbb{P}_{e}(S). (23)
Proof.

We prove this result by constructing an explicit coupling between 𝐗𝐇𝐤{\bf X_{H_{k}}} and 𝐗𝐒{\bf X_{S}}, demonstrating that 𝐗𝐒{\bf X_{S}} can be viewed as a degraded version of 𝐗𝐇𝐤{\bf X_{H_{k}}}.

We now define a bijection π:S→Hk\pi:S\to H_{k} satisfying ϵπ​(j)≤ϵj\epsilon_{\pi(j)}\leq\epsilon_{j}. To see this, first set π​(j)=j\pi(j)=j for all j∈S∩Hkj\in S\cap H_{k}. The remaining elements satisfy |S∖Hk|=|Hk∖S||S\setminus H_{k}|=|H_{k}\setminus S|, so we can bijectively map S∖HkS\setminus H_{k} onto Hk∖SH_{k}\setminus S. For any j∈S∖Hkj\in S\setminus H_{k} we have j>kj>k, hence ϵj≥ϵk≥ϵi\epsilon_{j}\geq\epsilon_{k}\geq\epsilon_{i} for every i∈Hki\in H_{k}, so in particular ϵπ​(j)≤ϵj\epsilon_{\pi(j)}\leq\epsilon_{j}.

Using the bijection π\pi from the step above, we now construct a random vector X~S=(X~j)j∈S\widetilde{X}_{S}=(\widetilde{X}_{j})_{j\in S} as follows: for each j∈Sj\in S, let i=π​(j)∈Hki=\pi(j)\in H_{k} be the paired index with ϵi≤ϵj\epsilon_{i}\leq\epsilon_{j}, compute the degradation parameter δj=ϵj−ϵi1−2​ϵi\delta_{j}=\frac{\epsilon_{j}-\epsilon_{i}}{1-2\epsilon_{i}} using Lemma A.3, and generate X~j\widetilde{X}_{j} by passing XiX_{i} through an independent BSC with crossover probability δj\delta_{j}. From Lemma A.3, each X~j\widetilde{X}_{j} has the same marginal distribution as XjX_{j}, i.e., it is the output of a BSC with error rate ϵj\epsilon_{j}. We now argue that 𝐗~𝐒=d𝐗𝐒{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle d}}{{=}}{\bf X_{S}}: each X~j\widetilde{X}_{j} has the correct marginal distribution (error rate ϵj\epsilon_{j}), the degradation noises are independent across components, and the original channels are conditionally independent given YY. This establishes that the joint distribution of X~S\widetilde{X}_{S} matches that of 𝐗S{\bf X}_{S}, i.e.,

𝐗~𝐒=d𝐗𝐒.{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle d}}{{=}}{\bf X_{S}}. (24)

By construction, X~S\widetilde{X}_{S} is obtained by applying independent noise to XHkX_{H_{k}}. Since YY is the common source and XHkX_{H_{k}} depends only on YY (through independent BSCs). As a result, we have the Markov chain:

Y⟶𝐗𝐇𝐤⟶𝐗~𝐒=d𝐗𝐒.Y\longrightarrow{\bf X_{H_{k}}}\longrightarrow{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle d}}{{=}}{\bf X_{S}}. (25)

Since we are able to construct the Markov chain above, now we can use the Data Processing Inequality(Cover and Thomas, 2006) which states that for any Markov chain A→B→CA\to B\to C, we have:

I​(A;C)≤I​(A;B).I(A;C)\leq I(A;B). (26)

Applying this to the Markov chain in Equation 25, we obtain:

I​(Y;𝐗𝐒)=I​(Y;𝐗~𝐒)≤I​(Y;𝐗𝐇𝐤),I(Y;{\bf X_{S}})=I(Y;{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}})\leq I(Y;{\bf X_{H_{k}}}), (27)

which proves (22).

Now, for the probability of error, we observe that any decision rule Y^​(𝐗𝐒)\hat{Y}({\bf X_{S}}) based on 𝐗𝐒{\bf X_{S}} can be replicated using 𝐗𝐇𝐤{\bf X_{H_{k}}} by first generating 𝐗~𝐒{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}} through the degradation construction and then applying the same decision rule. Formally, we have:

ℙe​(S)=minY^⁡ℙ​(Y^​(𝐗𝐒)≠Y)=minY^⁡ℙ​(Y^​(𝐗~𝐒)≠Y).\mathbb{P}_{e}(S)=\min_{\hat{Y}}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}({\bf X_{S}})\neq Y)=\min_{\hat{Y}}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}({\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}})\neq Y). (28)

Since 𝐗~𝐒{\bf\widetilde{X}_{S}} is a (possibly randomized) function of 𝐗𝐇𝐤{\bf X_{H_{k}}}, the optimal estimator based on 𝐗𝐇𝐤{\bf X_{H_{k}}} can only achieve lower or equal error:

ℙe​(Hk)=minY^⁡ℙ​(Y^​(𝐗𝐇𝐤)≠Y)≤ℙe​(S),\mathbb{P}_{e}(H_{k})=\min_{\hat{Y}}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}({\bf X_{H_{k}}})\neq Y)\leq\mathbb{P}_{e}(S), (29)

which proves (23). ∎

Consequently, we can make the following two intuitive remarks regarding this result:

Remark A.5 (Tightness of the Bound).

The inequalities in Theorem 4.1 become equalities whenever SS consists of kk channels with the smallest error rates (ties allowed). For any other subset S≠HkS\neq H_{k}, at least one component must be strictly degraded (i.e., δj>0\delta_{j}>0 for some jj), leading to a strict inequality.

Remark A.6 (Extension to Non-Uniform Priors).

The proof relies only on the Markov chain structure and the Data Processing Inequality, which hold regardless of the prior distribution on YY. Thus, Theorem 4.1 extends to any prior ℙ​(Y=1)=p∈(0,1)\mathbb{P}(Y=1)=p\in(0,1).

To visualize the stochastic degradation argument, we provide the following example.

Appendix B Proof of Theorem 4.3

We first establish the key technical tools used in the proof.

Definition B.1 (The Conditional Mutual Information).

The conditional mutual information between random variables XX and YY given ZZ is defined as:

I​(X;Y∣Z)≜H​(X∣Z)−H​(X∣Y,Z)=𝔼Z​[DKL​(PX,Y|Z∥PX|Z​PY|Z)].I(X;Y\mid Z)\triangleq H(X\mid Z)-H(X\mid Y,Z)=\mathbb{E}_{Z}\left[D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P_{X,Y|Z}\|P_{X|Z}P_{Y|Z})\right]. (30)

This quantity measures the information that YY provides about XX beyond what is already known from ZZ.

Lemma B.2 (The Chain Rule for Mutual Information).

For any random variables XX, YY, and ZZ, we have:

I​(X;Y,Z)=I​(X;Z)+I​(X;Y∣Z)=I​(X;Y)+I​(X;Z∣Y).I(X;Y,Z)=I(X;Z)+I(X;Y\mid Z)=I(X;Y)+I(X;Z\mid Y). (31)
Lemma B.3 (The Entropy Invariance Under Bijection).

Let XX be a random variable and ff be an arbitrary bijective function. Then, we have:

H​(f​(X))=H​(X).H(f(X))=H(X). (32)

More generally, for jointly distributed (X,Z)(X,Z) where f​(⋅;z)f(\cdot;z) is bijective for each fixed zz. Similarly, we have:

H​(f​(X;Z)∣Z)=H​(X∣Z).H(f(X;Z)\mid Z)=H(X\mid Z). (33)
Proof.

The bijection establishes a one-to-one correspondence between outcomes, preserving probability distributions, i.e., ℙ​(f​(X)=y)=ℙ​(X=f−1​(y))\mathbb{P}(f(X)=y)=\mathbb{P}(X=f^{-1}(y)). Since entropy depends only on the probability distribution, the result follows. ∎

For convenience, we first restate the decomposition theorem (Theorem 4.3) and then provide its proof.

Theorem 4.3 [Accuracy-Redundancy-Error Decomposition] Assume Y∈{±1}Y\in\{\pm 1\} and Xj∈{±1}X_{j}\in\{\pm 1\} for all jj. Let Ej=𝟙​(Xj≠Y)E_{j}=\mathds{1}(X_{j}\neq Y) be the error indicator for model jj, and define the marginal information gain of adding model jj to subset SS as:

Δ​(j∣S)≜I​(Y;Xj∣𝐗𝐒).\Delta(j\mid S)\triangleq I(Y;X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}}). (34)

Then, Δ​(j∣S)\Delta(j\mid S) admits the decomposition:

Δ​(j∣S)=I​(Y;Xj)⏟Accuracy−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)⏟Redundancy+I​(Ej;ES)⏟Error Correlation+Λj​(S)⏟Correction,\Delta(j\mid S)=\underbrace{I(Y;X_{j})}_{\text{Accuracy}}-\underbrace{I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}})}_{\text{Redundancy}}+\underbrace{I(E_{j};E_{S})}_{\text{Error Correlation}}+\underbrace{\Lambda_{j}(S)}_{\text{Correction}}, (35)

where the label-dependent correction term is given by:

Λj​(S)≜I​(Ej;Y∣ES)−I​(Ej;Y).\Lambda_{j}(S)\triangleq I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})-I(E_{j};Y). (36)
Proof.

We proceed through a series of algebraic manipulations using the chain rule and the entropy invariance properties.

By using the definition of the conditional mutual information, we have:

Δ​(j∣S)=I​(Y;Xj∣𝐗𝐒)=H​(Xj∣𝐗𝐒)−H​(Xj∣𝐗𝐒,Y).\Delta(j\mid S)=I(Y;X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}})=H(X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}})-H(X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}},Y). (37)

Applying the identity H​(X∣Z)=H​(X)−I​(X;Z)H(X\mid Z)=H(X)-I(X;Z), we have:

H​(Xj∣𝐗𝐒)=H​(Xj)−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒).H(X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}})=H(X_{j})-I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}}). (38)

Observe that conditioned on the true label YY, there exists a deterministic bijection between model outputs and error indicators. For labels Y,Xj∈{+1,−1}Y,X_{j}\in\{+1,-1\} and error indicator Ej∈{0,1}E_{j}\in\{0,1\}, this bijection is given by:

Xj=Y⋅(−1)Ej⟺Ej=𝟙​(Xj≠Y).X_{j}=Y\cdot(-1)^{E_{j}}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad E_{j}=\mathds{1}(X_{j}\neq Y). (39)

Explicitly, we have:

  • •

    If Ej=0E_{j}=0 (i.e., model jj’s estimate is correct), then Xj=YX_{j}=Y.

  • •

    If Ej=1E_{j}=1 (i.e., model jj’s estimate is incorrect), then Xj=−YX_{j}=-Y.

Given YY, knowing XjX_{j} uniquely determines EjE_{j}, and vice versa. This bijection extends component-wise: given YY, the vector 𝐗𝐒{\bf X_{S}} is in one-to-one correspondence with ESE_{S}.

From Lemma B.3, the entropy is preserved under this bijection which implies that:

H​(Xj∣𝐗𝐒,Y)=H​(Ej∣ES,Y).H(X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}},Y)=H(E_{j}\mid E_{S},Y). (40)

Using the chain rule for entropy, we obtain:

H​(Ej∣ES,Y)=H​(Ej∣Y)−I​(Ej;ES∣Y).H(E_{j}\mid E_{S},Y)=H(E_{j}\mid Y)-I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y). (41)

Substituting (38), (40), and (41) into (37), we have:

Δ​(j∣S)=H​(Xj)−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)−H​(Ej∣Y)+I​(Ej;ES∣Y).\Delta(j\mid S)=H(X_{j})-I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}})-H(E_{j}\mid Y)+I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y). (42)

By the bijection argument, H​(Xj∣Y)=H​(Ej∣Y)H(X_{j}\mid Y)=H(E_{j}\mid Y). Therefore:

I​(Y;Xj)=H​(Xj)−H​(Xj∣Y)=H​(Xj)−H​(Ej∣Y).I(Y;X_{j})=H(X_{j})-H(X_{j}\mid Y)=H(X_{j})-H(E_{j}\mid Y). (43)

Rearranging: H​(Xj)−H​(Ej∣Y)=I​(Y;Xj)H(X_{j})-H(E_{j}\mid Y)=I(Y;X_{j}).

Applying Lemma B.2 to I​(Ej;ES,Y)I(E_{j};E_{S},Y) in two ways:

I​(Ej;ES,Y)\displaystyle I(E_{j};E_{S},Y) =I​(Ej;ES)+I​(Ej;Y∣ES),\displaystyle=I(E_{j};E_{S})+I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S}), (44)
I​(Ej;ES,Y)\displaystyle I(E_{j};E_{S},Y) =I​(Ej;Y)+I​(Ej;ES∣Y).\displaystyle=I(E_{j};Y)+I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y). (45)

Equating Equation 44 and Equation 45 and solving for I​(Ej;ES∣Y)I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y):

I​(Ej;ES∣Y)=I​(Ej;ES)+[I​(Ej;Y∣ES)−I​(Ej;Y)]⏟Λj​(S).I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y)=I(E_{j};E_{S})+\underbrace{\left[I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})-I(E_{j};Y)\right]}_{\Lambda_{j}(S)}. (46)

Substituting Equation 43 and Equation 46 into Equation 42:

Δ​(j∣S)\displaystyle\Delta(j\mid S) =I​(Y;Xj)−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)+I​(Ej;ES∣Y)\displaystyle=I(Y;X_{j})-I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}})+I(E_{j};E_{S}\mid Y) (47)
=I​(Y;Xj)−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)+I​(Ej;ES)+Λj​(S).\displaystyle=I(Y;X_{j})-I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}})+I(E_{j};E_{S})+\Lambda_{j}(S). (48)

This completes the proof. ∎

Remark B.4 (Interpretation of Terms).

The decomposition in Equation 35 has an intuitive interpretation:

  • •

    Accuracy I​(Y;Xj)I(Y;X_{j}): The single predictive power of model jj.

  • •

    Redundancy I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}}): Information overlap between model jj and the existing ensemble SS.

  • •

    Error Correlation I​(Ej;ES)I(E_{j};E_{S}): dependence among model errors, capturing shared failure modes that influences the incremental value of adding model jj.

  • •

    Correction Λj​(S)\Lambda_{j}(S): Adjustment for label-dependent error behavior.

Corollary B.5 (Label-Invariant Case).

Under the label-invariant error assumption (E1,…,Em)⟂Y(E_{1},\ldots,E_{m})\perp Y, we have:

I​(Ej;Y)=0andI​(Ej;Y∣ES)=0,∀j,S.I(E_{j};Y)=0\quad\text{and}\quad I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})=0,\quad\forall j,S. (49)

Consequently, Λj​(S)=0\Lambda_{j}(S)=0 for all jj and SS, and the decomposition simplifies to:

Δ​(j∣S)=I​(Y;Xj)−I​(Xj;𝐗𝐒)+I​(Ej;ES).\Delta(j\mid S)=I(Y;X_{j})-I(X_{j};{\bf X_{S}})+I(E_{j};E_{S}). (50)
Proof.

Independence (E1,…,Em)⟂Y(E_{1},\ldots,E_{m})\perp Y implies I​(Ej;Y)=0I(E_{j};Y)=0 directly. For the conditional term, note that ESE_{S} is a function of (E1,…,Em)(E_{1},\ldots,E_{m}), so (Ej,ES)⟂Y(E_{j},E_{S})\perp Y, which implies I​(Ej;Y∣ES)=0I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})=0. ∎

Proposition B.6 (Bound on the Correction Term).

The label-dependence correction term satisfies:

−I​(Ej;Y)≤Λj​(S)≤H​(Y)−I​(Ej;Y).-I(E_{j};Y)\leq\Lambda_{j}(S)\leq H(Y)-I(E_{j};Y). (51)
Proof.

By non-negativity of mutual information, I​(Ej;Y∣ES)≥0I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})\geq 0. Therefore:

Λj​(S)=I​(Ej;Y∣ES)−I​(Ej;Y)≥−I​(Ej;Y).\Lambda_{j}(S)=I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})-I(E_{j};Y)\geq-I(E_{j};Y). (52)

Conditional mutual information is bounded by conditional entropy:

I​(Ej;Y∣ES)≤H​(Y∣ES)≤H​(Y).I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})\leq H(Y\mid E_{S})\leq H(Y). (53)

Therefore:

Λj​(S)=I​(Ej;Y∣ES)−I​(Ej;Y)≤H​(Y)−I​(Ej;Y).∎\Lambda_{j}(S)=I(E_{j};Y\mid E_{S})-I(E_{j};Y)\leq H(Y)-I(E_{j};Y).\qed (54)
Remark B.7 (Practical Implications).

For balanced binary classification with H​(Y)=1H(Y)=1 bit, |Λj​(S)|≤1|\Lambda_{j}(S)|\leq 1 bit. Moreover, if the joint label-dependence is small, e.g.,

I​(Ej,ES;Y)≤δ,I(E_{j},E_{S};Y)\leq\delta, (55)

then |Λj​(S)|≤δ|\Lambda_{j}(S)|\leq\delta and the correction vanishes as δ→0\delta\to 0. This motivates ignoring Λj​(S)\Lambda_{j}(S) when empirical estimates indicate weak joint dependence between error patterns and labels.

Appendix C Proof of Theorem 4.4

First, we restate the Gaussian-copula (one-factor) representation used in Section 3.1 and then give a step-by-step proof.

C.1 Model recap and notation

Let Y∈{±1}Y\in\{\pm 1\} be the binary label with a balanced prior ℙ​(Y=+1)=ℙ​(Y=−1)=1/2\mathbb{P}(Y=+1)=\mathbb{P}(Y=-1)=1/2. Let U∼𝒩​(0,1)U\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) be a latent common factor and let V1,V2,…V_{1},V_{2},\dots be i.i.d. 𝒩​(0,1)\mathcal{N}(0,1), independent of UU and YY. Fix ρ∈(0,1)\rho\in(0,1) and define latent Gaussian scores

Tj≜ρ​U+1−ρ​Vj,j=1,2,…T_{j}\triangleq\sqrt{\rho}\,U+\sqrt{1-\rho}\,V_{j},\qquad j=1,2,\dots (56)

so that (T1,…,Tm)(T_{1},\dots,T_{m}) is equicorrelated Gaussian with corr​(Ti,Tj)=ρ\mathrm{corr}(T_{i},T_{j})=\rho for i≠ji\neq j.

Define the error indicator by thresholding:

Ej≜𝟙​(Tj≤τ),E_{j}\triangleq\mathds{1}(T_{j}\leq\tau), (57)

and define the observed model output as a sign-flip of YY:

Xj≜Y⋅(−1)Ej.X_{j}\triangleq Y\cdot(-1)^{E_{j}}. (58)

The threshold τ\tau is chosen to match the marginal accuracy α>1/2\alpha>1/2: since Tj∼𝒩​(0,1)T_{j}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1) marginally, we have

ℙ​(Ej=1)=ℙ​(Tj≤τ)=Φ​(τ)=1−α.\mathbb{P}(E_{j}=1)=\mathbb{P}(T_{j}\leq\tau)=\Phi(\tau)=1-\alpha. (59)

Then, we can obtain τ=Φ−1​(1−α)\tau=\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha).

C.2 Proof of Theorem 4.4

Conditioned on U=uU=u, we have from (56) that

Tj∣(U=u)∼𝒩​(ρ​u, 1−ρ)independently across ​j.T_{j}\mid(U=u)\;\sim\;\mathcal{N}\!\big(\sqrt{\rho}\,u,\,1-\rho\big)\quad\text{independently across }j.

Therefore, conditioned on U=uU=u, the errors (Ej)j≥1(E_{j})_{j\geq 1} are i.i.d. Bernoulli with

p​(u)≜ℙ​(Ej=1∣U=u)=ℙ​(Tj≤τ∣U=u)=Φ​(τ−ρ​u1−ρ).p(u)\triangleq\mathbb{P}(E_{j}=1\mid U=u)=\mathbb{P}\!\left(T_{j}\leq\tau\mid U=u\right)=\Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau-\sqrt{\rho}\,u}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}\right). (60)

Now, let E¯m≜1m​∑j=1mEj\bar{E}_{m}\triangleq\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}E_{j}. By the Strong Law of Large Numbers applied conditional on UU, we have almost surely

E¯m→m→∞a.s.p​(U).\bar{E}_{m}\xrightarrow[m\to\infty]{a.s.}p(U). (61)

From (58), each Ej=1E_{j}=1 flips XjX_{j} to the wrong sign. Hence, among the mm outputs, the number of incorrect predictions equals ∑j=1mEj\sum_{j=1}^{m}E_{j}. Therefore, the majority vote is correct if and only if the number of total errors is fewer than half the entire models. Then, we have

Y^maj=Yif and only ifE¯m<12,\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{maj}}=Y\quad\text{if and only if}\quad\bar{E}_{m}<\tfrac{1}{2}, (62)

and fails if and only if E¯m>12\bar{E}_{m}>\tfrac{1}{2} (ties have vanishing probability as m→∞m\to\infty).

Since asymptotic failure event depends only on p​(U)p(U), by combining (61) and (62), we obtain the almost sure limit:

𝟙​{Y^maj≠Y}→m→∞a.s. 1​{p​(U)>12}.\mathds{1}\{\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{maj}}\neq Y\}\;\xrightarrow[m\to\infty]{a.s.}\;\mathds{1}\{p(U)>\tfrac{1}{2}\}. (63)

Taking expectations yields

limm→∞ℙ​(Y^maj≠Y)=ℙ​(p​(U)>12).\lim_{m\to\infty}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{maj}}\neq Y)=\mathbb{P}\!\left(p(U)>\tfrac{1}{2}\right). (64)

Also note that the MAP estimation coincides with the majority vote under the balanced, symmetric model; under the construction (58), the model is sign-symmetric: flipping (Y,X[m])(Y,X_{[m]}) to (−Y,−X[m])(-Y,-X_{[m]}) leaves the joint law invariant, and the prior on YY is balanced. Consequently, the MAP decision rule depends only on the majority sign of (X1,…,Xm)(X_{1},\dots,X_{m}) (and agrees with majority vote). Hence,

ℙ​(Y^MAP≠Y)=ℙ​(Y^maj≠Y)for each ​m,\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{MAP}}\neq Y)=\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{maj}}\neq Y)\quad\text{for each }m, (65)

and as a result, the same limit in (64) holds for Y^MAP\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{MAP}}.

Now let us find the probability of the event p​(U)>12p(U)>\tfrac{1}{2} explicitly. From (60), using that Φ​(⋅)\Phi(\cdot) is strictly increasing, we have p​(U)>12p(U)>\tfrac{1}{2} which is equivalent to having τ−ρ​U1−ρ>0\frac{\tau-\sqrt{\rho}\,U}{\sqrt{1-\rho}}>0. This condition implies

U<τρ.U<\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{\rho}}.

Since U∼𝒩​(0,1)U\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1), we obtain the following

ℙ​(p​(U)>12)=Φ​(τρ).\mathbb{P}\!\left(p(U)>\tfrac{1}{2}\right)=\Phi\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sqrt{\rho}}\right). (66)

Substituting τ=Φ−1​(1−α)\tau=\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha) using (59) in (66) gives

limm→∞ℙ​(Y^MAP≠Y)=Φ​(Φ−1​(1−α)ρ),\lim_{m\to\infty}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{MAP}}\neq Y)=\Phi\!\left(\frac{\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)}{\sqrt{\rho}}\right),

which is strictly positive for any ρ>0\rho>0 and α∈(1/2,1)\alpha\in(1/2,1). This completes the proof.

Remark C.1 (Edge cases and interpretation).

Because Φ−1​(1−α)<0\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)<0 for α>1/2\alpha>1/2, the error floor lies in (0,1/2)(0,1/2) for any ρ>0\rho>0. As ρ\rho decreases to 0, the expression Φ−1​(1−α)ρ\frac{\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)}{\sqrt{\rho}} tends to −∞-\infty and limm→∞ℙ​(Y^MAP≠Y)\lim_{m\to\infty}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{MAP}}\neq Y) vanishes, recovering the independent-error regime. As ρ\rho increases to 1, the expression Φ−1​(1−α)ρ\frac{\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)}{\sqrt{\rho}} approaches Φ−1​(1−α)\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha) which implies limm→∞ℙ​(Y^MAP≠Y)=1−α\lim_{m\to\infty}\mathbb{P}(\hat{Y}_{\mathrm{MAP}}\neq Y)=1-\alpha, matching the intuition that perfectly correlated models provide no ensemble gain.

Appendix D Difficulty-Aware Decomposition and the Price of Difficulty

To justify our greedy selection and to further explain Theorem 4.4, we expose a difficulty-aware submodular component and an intrinsic saturation penalty.

Theorem D.1 (Difficulty-Aware Decomposition and Price of Difficulty).

Let YY be the true label and X[m]={X1,…,Xm}X_{[m]}=\{X_{1},\dots,X_{m}\} be discrete model outputs (e.g., binary). Assume that there exists a latent variable DD (representing the prompt difficulty) such that:

  1. 1.

    D⟂YD\perp Y, i.e., DD and YY are statistically independent random variables,

  2. 2.

    for every S⊆[m]S\subseteq[m], model outputs are conditionally independent given (Y,D)(Y,D):

    ℙ​(𝐗𝐒∣Y,D)=∏j∈Sℙ​(Xj∣Y,D).\mathbb{P}({\bf X_{S}}\mid Y,D)=\prod_{j\in S}\mathbb{P}(X_{j}\mid Y,D). (67)

Then, for every S⊆[m]S\subseteq[m],

I​(Y;𝐗𝐒)=I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)−I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒).I(Y;{\bf X_{S}})=I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D)-I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}). (68)

We refer to I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D) as the difficulty-aware mutual information which quantifies how informative the subset would be if the latent difficulty DD were revealed to the decoder. We refer to I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒)I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}) as the Price of Difficulty which measures the remaining ambiguity that is related to not observing DD after seeing the ensemble.

Moreover, F​(S)≜I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)F(S)\triangleq I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D) is monotone submodular in SS (under a cardinality constraint |S|≤k|S|\leq k). Finally, the Price of Difficulty admits the form

I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒)=H​(Y∣𝐗𝐒)−H​(Y∣𝐗𝐒,D),I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}})=H(Y\mid{\bf X_{S}})-H(Y\mid{\bf X_{S}},D), (69)

i.e., the additional label uncertainty induced by not observing DD after seeing the ensemble.

Remark D.2.

Theorem D.1 isolates a difficulty-aware component I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D) that is monotone submodular, hence greedy achieves a (1−1/e)(1-1/e) approximation guarantee for maximizing this term under |S|≤k|S|\leq k (Krause and Golovin, 2014). In practice, DD is unobserved, and the difference between the difficulty-aware information and the true information is exactly the Price of Difficulty I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒)I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}). If this term does not vanish as |S||S| grows, then adding more models cannot fully remove uncertainty about YY, which provides an information-theoretic explanation for the saturation phenomenon characterized in Theorem 4.4.

D.1 Proof of Theorem D.1

For the proof, we use the conditional mutual information and chain rule as stated in: Definition B.1 and Lemma B.2. Now, lets proceed with the proof.

Proof.

We prove the decomposition, the operational form of the Price of Difficulty, and the monotone submodularity of F​(S)≜I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D).F(S)\triangleq I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D).

Let’s first derive the decomposition in Equation 68. We can apply the chain rule for mutual information to the triple (Y,𝐗𝐒,D)(Y,{\bf X_{S}},D) in two different ways:

I​(Y;𝐗𝐒,D)\displaystyle I(Y;{\bf X_{S}},D) =I​(Y;XS)+I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒),\displaystyle=I(Y;X_{S})+I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}), (70)
I​(Y;𝐗𝐒,D)\displaystyle I(Y;{\bf X_{S}},D) =I​(Y;D)+I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D).\displaystyle=I(Y;D)+I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D). (71)

Equating Equations 70 and 71 yields

I​(Y;𝐗𝐒)=I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)+I​(Y;D)−I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒).I(Y;{\bf X_{S}})=I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D)+I(Y;D)-I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}). (72)

By the assumption in the theorem, D⟂YD\perp Y, hence I​(Y;D)=0I(Y;D)=0. Substituting into Equation 72 gives I​(Y;𝐗𝐒)=I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)−I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒),I(Y;{\bf X_{S}})=I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D)-I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}}), which is exactly Equation 68.

Now, we can derive the Equation 69 by using the definition of conditional mutual information,

I​(Y;D∣𝐗𝐒)=H​(Y∣𝐗𝐒)−H​(Y∣𝐗𝐒,D),I(Y;D\mid{\bf X_{S}})=H(Y\mid{\bf X_{S}})-H(Y\mid{\bf X_{S}},D), (73)

which matches Equation 69.

To prove the monotone submodularity of F​(S)=I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)F(S)=I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D), we use an entropy representation:

F​(S)=I​(Y;𝐗𝐒∣D)=H​(𝐗𝐒∣D)−H​(𝐗𝐒∣Y,D).F(S)=I(Y;{\bf X_{S}}\mid D)=H({\bf X_{S}}\mid D)-H({\bf X_{S}}\mid Y,D). (74)

Under the second assumption, the outputs factorize given (Y,D)(Y,D): ℙ​(𝐗𝐒∣Y,D)=∏j∈Sℙ​(Xj∣Y,D).\mathbb{P}({\bf X_{S}}\mid Y,D)=\prod_{j\in S}\mathbb{P}(X_{j}\mid Y,D). Consequently, the conditional entropy decomposes additively as

H​(𝐗𝐒∣Y,D)=∑j∈SH​(Xj∣Y,D),H({\bf X_{S}}\mid Y,D)=\sum_{j\in S}H(X_{j}\mid Y,D), (75)

so the set function S↦H​(𝐗𝐒∣Y,D)S\mapsto H({\bf X_{S}}\mid Y,D) is modular under the conditional independence given (Y,D)(Y,D). Next, for discrete random variables, Shannon entropy is submodular in its set argument, and conditioning preserves submodularity; therefore S↦H​(𝐗𝐒∣D)S\mapsto H({\bf X_{S}}\mid D) is submodular (see, e.g., (Cover and Thomas, 2006)). Recalling the identity F​(S)F(S) in (74), we conclude that FF is submodular as a submodular function minus a modular one. Finally, for any j∉Sj\notin S,

F​(S∪{j})−F​(S)=I​(Y;Xj∣𝐗𝐒,D)≥0,F(S\cup\{j\})-F(S)=I(Y;X_{j}\mid{\bf X_{S}},D)\geq 0, (76)

where the last inequality follows from non-negativity of conditional mutual information. Therefore, FF is monotone.

∎

Appendix E Implementation Details

In this section, we detail the implementation of the algorithms used throughout the experiments. We first present the smoothed mutual information estimation method, followed by the pseudo codes for the MAP, Majority Voting, and Weighted Majority Voting aggregation rules. We conclude with providing the implementation details for the Gaussian-copula parameter estimation that is used for modeling correlated errors.

E.1 Mutual Information Estimation

Algorithm 2 summarizes how we estimate mutual information from finite samples. Given NN paired observations of discrete variables AA and BB, we form empirical marginal and joint counts and convert them to probabilities using Laplace (add-one) smoothing with α=1\alpha=1 (please note that this parameter is for smoothing, not for the accuracy as used in Theorem 4.4). This guarantees strictly positive probability estimates, so the entropy terms are always well-defined (no log⁡0\log 0), and it stabilizes the estimates when some outcomes are rare or absent in the parameter-estimation split (the 80% portion used in each run and split). The mutual information is then computed via the standard identity I​(A;B)=H​(A)+H​(B)−H​(A,B)I(A;B)=H(A)+H(B)-H(A,B), where each entropy term is estimated from the smoothed distributions.

Computational complexity: Let KA=|𝒜|K_{A}=|\mathcal{A}| and KB=|ℬ|K_{B}=|\mathcal{B}|. Algorithm 2 estimates the marginals in 𝒪​(N+KA+KB)\mathcal{O}(N+K_{A}+K_{B}) time (one pass to count plus normalization), estimates the joint in 𝒪​(N+KA​KB)\mathcal{O}(N+K_{A}K_{B}) time, and computes entropies in 𝒪​(KA+KB+KA​KB)\mathcal{O}(K_{A}+K_{B}+K_{A}K_{B}) time. Overall, the time complexity is

𝒪​(N+KA​KB),\mathcal{O}\bigl(N+K_{A}K_{B}\bigr),

and the memory cost is 𝒪​(KA​KB)\mathcal{O}(K_{A}K_{B}) to store the smoothed joint table (plus 𝒪​(KA+KB)\mathcal{O}(K_{A}+K_{B}) for marginals). In our setting A,BA,B are low-cardinality (e.g., |𝒜|=|ℬ|=2|\mathcal{A}|=|\mathcal{B}|=2 for binary variables), so the MI estimation is negligible compared to LLM query costs.

E.2 Aggregation Methods

Algorithm 3 summarizes our MAP aggregator. For a selected subset SS of size kk, we estimate P​(Y∣XS)P(Y\mid X_{S}) from the training split by counting labels for each prediction pattern xS∈{−1,+1}kx_{S}\in\{-1,+1\}^{k} (there are 2k2^{k} such patterns). We apply Laplace smoothing (add-one) to avoid zero counts for rare or unseen patterns. At test time, given the observed pattern, we predict the label with the larger smoothed count, which implements the MAP rule under a uniform prior. This estimator makes no conditional-independence assumption; it uses the empirical joint distribution of XSX_{S}.

Computational complexity: Let k=|S|k=|S|. The plug-in MAP aggregator maintains two count tables (Cℓ+,Cℓ−)ℓ=12k(C^{+}_{\ell},C^{-}_{\ell})_{\ell=1}^{2^{k}}, one entry per prediction pattern. On the training split, computing the pattern index for each example takes 𝒪​(k)\mathcal{O}(k) time and updating the corresponding counts is 𝒪​(1)\mathcal{O}(1), for a total of 𝒪​(Ntr​k)\mathcal{O}(N_{\mathrm{tr}}\,k) time. Initializing the count tables costs 𝒪​(2k)\mathcal{O}(2^{k}). At test time, each example again requires 𝒪​(k)\mathcal{O}(k) time to compute its index and 𝒪​(1)\mathcal{O}(1) time to compare (Cℓ+,Cℓ−)(C^{+}_{\ell},C^{-}_{\ell}), yielding 𝒪​(Nte​k)\mathcal{O}(N_{\mathrm{te}}\,k) time. Overall, the time complexity is 𝒪​((Ntr+Nte)​k+2k)\mathcal{O}((N_{\mathrm{tr}}+N_{\mathrm{te}})\,k+2^{k}) and the memory complexity is 𝒪​(2k)\mathcal{O}(2^{k}). In our experiments k≤13k\leq 13, so 2k≤81922^{k}\leq 8192 and the aggregation overhead is negligible compared to LLM query cost.

Algorithm 4 implements the standard majority voting aggregation. Given predictions from the selected subset SS, we sum the binary votes Xj∈{−1,+1}X_{j}\in\{-1,+1\} and predict the sign of the total. When the ensemble size |S||S| is odd, ties cannot occur; for even |S||S|, ties are broken randomly. This approach treats all models equally regardless of their individual accuracies, which can be suboptimal when model quality varies significantly.

Algorithm 5 extends majority voting by weighting each model’s vote according to its accuracy. If pjp_{j} represents the model jj being correct, then weight wj=log⁡(pj/(1−pj))w_{j}=\log(p_{j}/(1-p_{j})) corresponds to the log-odds of model jj being correct, which is optimal when model errors are independent (for the proof, see the Theorem E.1 and related proof below). Note that in our experiments we estimate the weights by calculating pjp_{j} using the training set that is obtained by splitting the data for each run.

Theorem E.1 (Optimality of Log-Odds Weights).

Consider a binary classification problem with a true label Y∈{−1,+1}Y\in\{-1,+1\} and an ensemble of MM independent classifiers. Let HiH_{i} be the random variable representing the output of classifier ii, such that its accuracy is given by ℙ​(Hi=Y)=pi\mathbb{P}(H_{i}=Y)=p_{i}.

Under the assumption of independence, the decision rule that minimizes the probability of error is the Weighted Majority Vote:

Y^=sign​(∑i=1Mwi​Hi)\hat{Y}=\mathrm{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M}w_{i}H_{i}\right)

where the optimal weight for each classifier is the log-odds of its accuracy:

wi=log⁡(pi1−pi)w_{i}=\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right)
Proof.

To minimize the probability of error, we use the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) decision rule. We choose the prediction Y^=+1\hat{Y}=+1 if the posterior probability satisfies:

ℙ​(Y=+1∣𝐇)>ℙ​(Y=−1∣𝐇)\mathbb{P}(Y=+1\mid\mathbf{H})>\mathbb{P}(Y=-1\mid\mathbf{H})

where 𝐇=(H1,…,HM)\mathbf{H}=(H_{1},\dots,H_{M}) denotes the vector of classifier outputs. Using Bayes’ Theorem (Hajek, 2020) and assuming a uniform prior ℙ​(Y=+1)=ℙ​(Y=−1)\mathbb{P}(Y=+1)=\mathbb{P}(Y=-1), this is equivalent to comparing the likelihoods:

ℙ​(𝐇∣Y=+1)ℙ​(𝐇∣Y=−1)>1\frac{\mathbb{P}(\mathbf{H}\mid Y=+1)}{\mathbb{P}(\mathbf{H}\mid Y=-1)}>1

Since the classifiers are assumed to be conditionally independent given the class label YY, the joint likelihood factors into a product:

∏i=1Mℙ​(Hi∣Y=+1)∏i=1Mℙ​(Hi∣Y=−1)>1\frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M}\mathbb{P}(H_{i}\mid Y=+1)}{\prod_{i=1}^{M}\mathbb{P}(H_{i}\mid Y=-1)}>1

Taking the logarithm of both sides yields the sum of log-likelihood ratios:

∑i=1Mlog⁡(ℙ​(Hi∣Y=+1)ℙ​(Hi∣Y=−1))>0\sum_{i=1}^{M}\log\left(\frac{\mathbb{P}(H_{i}\mid Y=+1)}{\mathbb{P}(H_{i}\mid Y=-1)}\right)>0

We analyze the term for the ii-th classifier based on its output realization HiH_{i}:

  1. 1.

    Case Hi=+1H_{i}=+1: The probability of this output given Y=+1Y=+1 is pip_{i} (correct), and given Y=−1Y=-1 is 1−pi1-p_{i} (error). The term becomes log⁡(pi1−pi)\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right).

  2. 2.

    Case Hi=−1H_{i}=-1: The probability of this output given Y=+1Y=+1 is 1−pi1-p_{i} (error), and given Y=−1Y=-1 is pip_{i} (correct). The term becomes log⁡(1−pipi)=−log⁡(pi1−pi)\log\left(\frac{1-p_{i}}{p_{i}}\right)=-\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right).

Thus, the log-likelihood ratio term for classifier ii can be written generally as Hi⋅log⁡(pi1−pi)H_{i}\cdot\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right). Substituting this back into the sum, the decision rule becomes:

∑i=1MHi​log⁡(pi1−pi)>0\sum_{i=1}^{M}H_{i}\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right)>0

This is equivalent to the weighted majority vote sign​(∑i=1Mwi​Hi)\mathrm{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M}w_{i}H_{i}\right) with weights wi=log⁡(pi1−pi)w_{i}=\log\left(\frac{p_{i}}{1-p_{i}}\right). ∎

E.3 Gaussian-Copula Fitting and Sampling Algorithm

Algorithm 6 explains Gaussian-copula fitting. To do that, first we estimate marginal error probabilities ϵ^j\hat{\epsilon}_{j} using the whole dataset (we don’t use splitting in here, since our goal is showing how Gaussian-copula is a trustable method for modeling the joint distributions), then compute latent thresholds τj=Φ−1​(ϵ^j)\tau_{j}=\Phi^{-1}(\hat{\epsilon}_{j}) where Φ\Phi is the normal CDF function.

Next, we capture dependencies using Tetrachoric correlation method (Bonett and Price, 2005). For each pair, we solve for the latent correlation ρj​k\rho_{jk} that matches the observed joint error probability: Φρj​k​(τj,τk)=ℙ​(Ej=1,Ek=1)\Phi_{\rho_{jk}}(\tau_{j},\tau_{k})=\mathbb{P}(E_{j}=1,E_{k}=1). Since pairwise estimation may yield a non-positive semi-definite matrix, we apply spectral projection by clipping negative eigenvalues to ϵ\epsilon and re-normalizing the diagonals. Finally, we generate synthetic errors by sampling latent vectors Z∼𝒩​(𝟎,𝚺^)Z\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},{\bf\hat{\Sigma}}) and applying the thresholding rule Esyn,i​j=𝟙​(Zi​j<τj)E_{\text{syn},ij}=\mathds{1}(Z_{ij}<\tau_{j}).

Appendix F Additional Experimental Details for the MEDMCQA Dataset

In this section, we provide more context on the MEDMCQA experiments.

For the binary evaluation task, we utilize the following prompt template to enforce a consistent ‘True’ or ‘False’ output format:

We accessed all language models through OpenRouter to standardize the inference process. An actual question from MEDMCQA, is illustrated below with ground truth “TRUE”:

F.1 Experiments across Temperature and Runs (the MAP aggregation)

We also provide the experiments for all temperature settings and runs with 5 random splits for each. Figure 7 reports per-condition curves on MEDMCQA under the MAP aggregation for each baseline, with one panel for average of each temperature–run–(5-split) pair. Table 4 aggregates the same experiments over all 3030 evaluations (3 temperatures ×\times 2 runs ×\times 5 random 80/20 splits), reporting mean ±\pm std test error at each ensemble size kk.

F.2 Alternative Aggregation Rules

Our main experiments use the MAP aggregator for the selected subset SS (Algorithm 3), since it directly estimates ℙ​(Y∣XS)\mathbb{P}(Y\mid X_{S}) and can exploit higher-order dependencies in correlated outputs. To probe robustness to the aggregation rule, we additionally evaluate two widely-used alternatives for the baseline selectors: (i) majority vote (MV) (Algorithm 4), and (ii) weighted majority vote (W-MV) (Algorithm 5), where weights are derived from single-model reliability on the training split. Note that in these comparisons, we keep selection fixed (Top-kk, Term 1, Terms 1+2) and only change the aggregation rule for those baselines. Greedy MI continues to use MAP, because its gains are designed to be realized under a likelihood-based estimator.

With the help of visualizations from Figure 8, and tables from Table 5, we observe that the mRMR selector (Terms 1+2) is highly unstable under MV: at k=2k=2 it jumps to 0.2640.264 error, versus 0.1810.181 for Top-kk and Term 1, and 0.1710.171 for Greedy MI (MAP). The instability persists across the budget range, with Terms 1+2 attaining 0.2310.231 error at k=3k=3 and 0.2200.220 at k=5k=5, while Greedy MI achieves 0.1640.164 and 0.1630.163, respectively. In the mid-budget regime (k=3k=3–77), Greedy MI consistently maintains the lowest error (e.g., k=5k=5: 0.1630.163 vs. 0.1700.170 for Top-kk; k=6k=6: 0.1640.164 vs. 0.1700.170 for Top-kk).

Under W-MV, in Figure 9, and in Table 6, we see that Terms 1+2 remains substantially worse in the mid-budget range (e.g., k=4k=4: 0.2040.204; k=6k=6: 0.1970.197), whereas Greedy MI (MAP) achieves 0.163±0.0080.163\pm 0.008 at k=4k=4 and 0.1640.164 at k=6k=6. Notably, Top-kk with W-MV performs comparably to Top-kk with MAP, suggesting that weighted voting partially compensates for ignoring correlations in the selection phase. However, it does not resolve the failure mode of mRMR, which continues to suffer from its aggressive diversity-seeking strategy. At larger budgets (k≥10k\geq 10), all methods begin to converge as the ensemble includes most available models. In panels (b), (c), (d), (e) and (f), baselines with WE aggregation occasionally outperform Greedy MI with MAP for large ensemble sizes. Two factors contribute to this situation: first, Greedy MI provides a near-optimal rather than optimal selection; second, as discussed previously, estimating the joint distribution becomes increasingly difficult at higher k, degrading Greedy MI’s performance. A well-chosen aggregation rule can partially compensate for these effects.

Overall, these results indicate that changing the aggregation rule can partially improve Top-kk/Term 1, particularly under W-MV, but it does not resolve the fundamental issues with mRMR under MV/W-MV. Greedy MI remains most competitive in the moderate-budget regime (k≈3k\approx 3–77) where selection quality matters most.

F.3 Copula Validation Plots across Temperatures and Runs

Additionally, we provide the following scatter and tail plots for each temperature setting and run pair below.

Figure 10 compares the empirical pairwise joint error probabilities P​(Ei∩Ej)P(E_{i}\cap E_{j}) with copula-predicted values across all (122)=66\binom{12}{2}=66 model pairs. The points align closely along the diagonal across all six temperature-run conditions, indicating strong agreement between the fitted copula and observed data.

Figure 11 evaluates higher-order structure by showing the distribution of simultaneous errors that is, how many models fail on the same instance. The copula-generated distributions (orange bars) match the empirical frequencies (blue bars) well across all settings.

Appendix G Additional Experimental Details for the MMLU Dataset

In this section, we provide more context on the MMLU experiments. For the binary evaluation task, we utilize the following prompt template to enforce a consistent ‘True’ or ‘False’ output format:

We accessed all language models through OpenRouter to standardize the inference process. An actual question from MMLU, is illustrated below with the ground truth “FALSE”:

G.1 Experiments across Temperature and Runs (the MAP Aggregation)

For MMLU, we also report the experiments for all temperature settings and runs with 5 random splits for each. Figure 12 reports per-condition curves on MMLU under the MAP aggregation for each baseline, with one panel for average of each temperature–run–(5-split) pair. Table 8 aggregates the same experiments over all 3030 evaluations (3 temperatures ×\times 2 runs ×\times 5 random 80/20 splits), reporting mean ±\pm std test error at each ensemble size kk.

G.2 Experiments with Alternative Aggregation Rules

In this part, we follow the same approach as in Appendix F.2. With the help of visualizations from Figure 13, and tables from Table 9, we observe that at small budgets, the mRMR selector (Terms 1+2) is highly unstable under MV: at k=2k=2 it jumps to 0.2460.246 error, versus 0.1560.156 for Top-kk and Term 1, and 0.1510.151 for Greedy MI (MAP). The same pattern persists at k=3k=3, where Terms 1+2 attains 0.2190.219 error while Greedy MI achieves 0.1480.148. In the mid-budget regime where our gains are largest, Greedy MI remains consistently lower (e.g., k=4k=4: 0.1440.144 vs. 0.1500.150 for Top-kk; k=6k=6: 0.1410.141 vs. 0.1450.145 for Top-kk).

Under W-MV, in Figure 14, and in Table 10, we see that Terms 1+2 remains substantially worse in the mid-budget range (e.g., k=4k=4: 0.1740.174; k=6k=6: 0.1670.167), whereas Greedy MI (MAP) achieves 0.144±0.0070.144\pm 0.007 at k=4k=4 and 0.1410.141 at k=6k=6. At larger budgets, W-MV can narrow the gap for simpler selectors: for example, Top-kk reaches 0.1530.153 at k=10k=10 (and 0.1560.156 at k=11k=11), comparable to Greedy MI (MAP) at 0.1550.155 (k=10k=10) and 0.1580.158 (k=11k=11). Overall, these results indicate that changing the aggregation rule can partially improve Top-kk/Term 1 at large kk, but it does not resolve the failure mode of mRMR under MV/W-MV; Greedy MI remains most competitive in the moderate-budget regime (k≈3k\approx 3–77) where selection quality matters most. Additionally, as ensemble size increases, panel (c), (d) and (e) show instances where baselines with WE aggregation surpass Greedy MI with MAP. This can be understood through two complementary factors: greedy selection, while effective, provides only near-optimal guarantees, and the difficulty of estimating high-dimensional joint distributions grows with k. When selection quality degrades, a powerful aggregation rule can help bridge the gap.

G.3 Copula Validation across Temperature and Runs

In this part, we validate that the Gaussian-copula model accurately captures the empirical error structure on MMLU across all temperature settings and runs. Figure 15 presents scatter plots comparing the empirical pairwise joint error probabilities P​(Ei∩Ej)P(E_{i}\cap E_{j}) against the copula-predicted values for all (132)=78\binom{13}{2}=78 model pairs. Across all six conditions, the points cluster tightly around the diagonal (ideal fit) with only a few of outlier clusters.

Figure 16 examines higher-order dependencies by plotting the distribution of simultaneous errors that is, the number of models that makes an error on the same instance. This diagnostic is critical for ensemble performance: if all models fail together on hard examples, no aggregation rule can recover. The copula-predicted distributions (orange bars) closely match the observed frequencies (blue bars) across all temperature-run pairs.

Appendix H Experimental Details for the IMDB Movie Reviews Dataset

In this section, we provide more context on the IMDB experiments. We employ the following 12 models: GPT-5-chat, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-4.1-nano from OpenAI (OpenAI, 2026), LLaMA-3.1-8b (Grattafiori et al., 2024), Qwen3-235b (Yang et al., 2025a), Mistral-small-3.1-24b and Mistral-small-3.2-24b (Mistral AI, 2026), Claude-4.5-haiku, Claude-4.5-sonnet (Anthropic, 2026), deepseek-chat-v3 (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025)

We run experiments at three temperature settings (0.01, 0.3, and 0.7), performing two independent runs at each temperature. We report the individual model accuracies and pairwise correlations in Figure 24 and in Table 15. Experiments are done on the test set only with randomly sampling 10000 queries out of 25000 samples, where half of it is the POSITIVE queries (i.e., 5000 samples) and other half is the NEGATIVE queries (i.e., 5000 samples). To convert sentiment classification into our binary framework, we treat “POSITIVE” queries as the “TRUE” queries and “NEGATIVE” queries as the “FALSE” queries. As a result, we utilize the following prompt template:

We accessed all language models through OpenRouter to standardize the inference process. An actual query from IMBD, is illustrated below with ground truth “FALSE”:

H.1 Experiments Across Temperatures and Runs (MAP aggregation)

We provide the experiments for all temperature settings and runs with 5 random splits for each. Figure 17 reports per-condition curves on the IMDB under the MAP aggregation for each baseline, with one panel for average of each temperature–run–(5-split) pair. Table 12 aggregates the same experiments over all 3030 evaluations (3 temperatures ×\times 2 runs ×\times 5 random 80/20 splits), reporting mean ±\pm std test error at each ensemble size kk. Unlike MEDMCQA and MMLU, the IMDB dataset exhibits uniformly high model accuracies (91–96%) and very strong pairwise correlations (ρ¯=0.90\bar{\rho}=0.90). As a result, the improvements from ensemble selection are modest and often within noise margins. This behavior is consistent with Theorem 4.4 because when correlations are near-uniform and high, the error floor Φ​(Φ−1​(1−α)/ρ)\Phi(\Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)/\sqrt{\rho}) leaves little room for improvement. Moreover, the sentiment classification inherently contains ambiguous instances (such as sarcastic or satirical reviews where the true sentiment is genuinely unclear) that all models tend to misclassify together. These “intrinsically difficult” samples create a shared failure mode that no selection strategy can overcome, further limiting the gains from ensembling.

H.2 Experiments with Alternative Aggregation Rules

Figures 18 and 19 and Tables 13 and 14 show MV and W-MV ablations for IMDB. Unlike MEDMCQA and MMLU, all methods perform comparably here, i.e., the curves largely overlap and differences fall within the variance bands.

H.3 Copula Validation

Figure 20 shows the pairwise fit remains tight along the diagonal across all settings. Figure 21 reveals that the error distribution is heavily concentrated at k=0k=0 (reflecting the high model accuracies) with the copula accurately capturing this structure. The near-absence of simultaneous errors (k≥2k\geq 2) further explains why ensemble selection yields limited gains on this dataset.
